channel.title: Diagnostics on Welcome to Christophe Nasarre's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Diagnostics on Welcome to Christophe Nasarre's Blog

item.title: How to support .NET Framework PDB format and source line with ISymUnmanagedReader
item.description: After DIA and DbgHelp, time to dig into ISymUnmanagedReader to get its line in source code.

item.title: Vibe coding a .pdb dumper or how I became a Product Manager
item.description: Follow me Vibe Coding in Cursor during my Datadog R&D Week to list function symbols with their signature and more

item.title: How to monitor .NET applications startup
item.description: This episode explains how to monitor the startup of a .NET application and get insights about its lock and wait contentions duration

item.title: Measuring the impact of locks and waits on latency in your .NET apps
item.description: Monitor mutex, semaphore and event wait duration

item.title: Implementing dotnet-http to monitor your HTTP requests
item.description: This episode shows how to listen to HTTP related events and compute the duration of DNS, socket and security phases

item.title: Digging into the undocumented .NET events from the BCL
item.description: This is the first episode of a new series describing how to listen to undocumented .NET events and focusing on monitoring HTTP requests

item.title: Unexpected usage of EventSource or how to test statistical results in CLR pull request
item.description: Unexpected usage of EventSource

item.title: Tips and tricks from validating a Pull Request in .NET CLR
item.description: Performance and validation in a .NET CLR pull request

item.title: Trigger your GCs with dotnet-fullgc!
item.description: This post explains how I wrote a CLI tool that triggers a GC in any .NET application

item.title: View your GCs statistics live with dotnet-gcstats!
item.description: Discover how to look at the .NET GC statistics to better understand your garbage collections

item.title: .NET .gcdump Internals
item.description: Learn what the .NET CLR does behind the scene to help the tools to generate a .gcdump!

item.title: Raiders of the lost root: looking for memory leaks in .NET
item.description: This post explains how you could write your own memory profiler based on new .NET 7.0 profiler APIs in C++

item.title: From Metadata to Event block in nettrace format
item.description: The previous episodes started the parsing of the “nettrace” format. This last episode covers Metadata and Event blocks format.

item.title: Reading “object” in memory — starting with stacks
item.description: During the parsing of the nettrace format, blocks are serialized as “object”. Let’s look at “stack” objects.

item.title: Parsing the “nettrace” stream of (not only) events
item.description: The previous episodes explained how to initiate the protocol to receive CLR events. It is now time to dig into the “nettrace” stream…

item.title: CLR events: go for the nettrace file format!
item.description: Let’s see how to listen to CLR event through EventPipe in C++. This episode explains how to start/stop a session with a running .NET app.

item.title: .NET Diagnostic IPC protocol: the C++ way
item.description: This post describes how to communicate in ++ with the diagnostic server in the CLR of a running .NET application.

item.title: Digging into the CLR Diagnostics IPC Protocol in C#
item.description: Learn how to directly connect to the .NET CLR and send diagnostics commands

item.title: Troubleshooting CPU and exceptions issues with Datadog toolbox
item.description: Learn how to use Datadog CPU and exceptions profiling to troubleshoot well known Tess Ferrandez BuggyBits

item.title: Value types and exceptions in .NET profiling
item.description: This final episode describes how to get fields of a value type instance and how deal with exceptions.

item.title: Troubleshooting .NET performance issues with Datadog toolbox
item.description: How to troubleshoot well known Tess Ferrandez BuggyBits application with the Datadog toolbox including the new Continuous Profiler

item.title: Profile memory allocations with Perfview
item.description: How to connect AllocationTick events to the corresponding callstacks thanks to the Microsoft Perfview free tool.

item.title: How to ease async callstacks analysis in Perfview
item.description: Our new post describes how to easily profile with Perfview and more interestingly, how to get human readable async callstacks!

item.title: Understanding “reversed” callstacks in Visual Studio and Perfview with async/await code
item.description: This post explains why profilers like Visual Studio could display “reversed” callstacks when dealing with async/await code.

item.title: Build your own .NET CPU profiler in C#
item.description: After describing memory allocation profiling it is now time to dig into the CPU sample profiling in C#!

item.title: How to write your own commands in dotnet-dump (2/2)
item.description: This post describes the different steps, tips and tricks to write your own commands for dotnet-dump

item.title: How to extend dotnet-dump (1/2) — What are the new commands?
item.description: This first post describes the new commands, when to use them, and the git setup I used to implement them.

item.title: The .NET Core Journey at Criteo
item.description: This post shows the challenges we faced during the migration to .NET Core on containerized Linux for our main application.

item.title: Build your own .NET memory profiler in C# — call stacks (2/2–1)
item.description: This post explains how to get the call stack corresponding to the allocations with CLR events.

item.title: Build your own .NET memory profiler in C# — Allocations (1/2)
item.description: This post explains how to collect allocation details by writing your own memory profiler in C#.

item.title: Debugging Wednesday at Criteo — Cancel this task!
item.description: Last Wednesday “Debugging” day for Kevin and myself. Let’s share with you these frustrating but interesting minutes.

item.title: Getting another view on thread stacks with ClrMD
item.description: This post of the series details how to look into your threads stack with ClrMD to get method calls, parameters and local variables.

item.title: WSL + Visual Studio = attaching/launching a Linux .NET Core application on my Window 10
item.description: This post shows how to attach to a .NET Core process running on Linux with WSL and also how to start a Linux process with Visual Studio…

item.title: How to expose your custom counters in .NET Core
item.description: This post shows how to code your own counters: you’ll get the count and duration of ASP.NET requests processed with(out) GC as example.

item.title: .NET Core Counters internals: how to integrate counters in your monitoring pipeline
item.description: This post shows how to easily get .NET Core counters. Their internals are also detailed for a better understanding of usage/limits

item.title: Spying on .NET Garbage Collector with .NET Core EventPipes
item.description: This post shows how to use .NET Core EventPipes to build Garbage Collector logs. The emitted raw CLR events are described in details.

item.title: Let’s debug the Core CLR with WinDBG!
item.description: This post of the series shows how you could easily debug the Core CLR in a real world case of insane thread contention duration.

item.title: Debugging Friday — Hunting down race condition
item.description: Another .NET investigation of our Debugging team to learn how to leverage tracepoint, object naming or parallel stacks in Visual Studio

item.title: Building your own Java-like GC logs in .NET
item.description: This post of the series focuses on logging each GC details in a file and how to leverage it during investigations.

item.title: Spying on .NET Garbage Collector with TraceEvent
item.description: This post of the series focuses on CLR events related to garbage collection in .NET.

item.title: In-process CLR event listeners with .NET Core 2.2
item.description: Write your own EventListener class

item.title: [C#] Get-process-name challenge on a Friday afternoon
item.description: Unexpected CPU consumption
